title: 3 Legal Considerations for Cheque Imaging
body_text:
3	Legal Considerations for Cheque Imaging
3.1	Overview of Legal Considerations
Financial institutions are responsible for complying with applicable legislation and regulations. The information provided in this Chapter is for convenience only and does not take the place of statutes and regulations. Reference should be made to the actual statute or regulation for the full text. There is no guarantee of accuracy for the online statutes.
Note:	Other federal and provincial legislation can influence cheque imaging (e.g.,
Personal Information Protection Act
(BC),
Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
. To address this issue, the financial institution must apply additional policies and procedures.
3.2	CPA Rules
The Canadian Payment Association (CPA) develops and implements the rules that apply to the clearing and settlement of different types of payments between its member financial institutions. Refer to the CPA website for more information on CPA Rules.
CPA Rule A1, General Rules Pertaining to Items Acceptable for Exchange for the Purpose of Clearing and Settlement
ГўВЂВ“ Outlines the general requirements for processing Canadian dollar items and establishes the amount limit for paper items. Paper items that equal or exceed $25 million cannot be exchanged through the Clearing on the same day.
CPA Rule A4, Returned and Redirected Items
ГўВЂВ“ Outlines the dishonour and return of items, including return reasons and timeframes.
CPA Rule A10, Images, Image Printouts and Return Replacement Documents
ГўВЂВ“ Outlines the requirements applicable to the creation and storage of Images and the use of Image Printouts or Return Replacement Documents for the purpose of return. Cheque Images have the same legal standing as physical cleared cheques.
CPA Standards 006, Specifications for MICR-Encoded Payment Items
ГўВЂВ“ Defines the location and background design of the necessary data elements for imageable MICR-encoded payment items and other MICR-encoded payment items.
3.3	Bills of Exchange Act
The
Bills of Exchange Act
governs the form of and dealings with financial instruments such as bills of exchange, cheques, and promissory notes. The
Bills of Exchange Act
regulates financial issues such as the time permitted for the dishonour and return of cheques, forged endorsements, and rights and powers of the bill of exchange holder. Refer to the Cheques Part for more information.
END
